pull_request.title: feat: expose stateful functions in SWIG bindings
pull_request.body:
## Summary

- Remove `%ignore` directives for `xtract_last_n`, `xtract_last_n_state_new`, and `xtract_last_n_state_delete`
- SWIG handles the opaque `xtract_last_n_state *` pointer natively — no special typemaps or workarounds needed
- Add stateful function tests to both Python and Java test scripts

The fix was literally deleting 3 lines. The `%ignore` was a workaround from 2013 that was never revisited.

Closes #59

## Test plan

- [x] `make && make check` passes (375 assertions, 61 test cases)
- [x] `make -C swig check` — Python and Java tests pass including stateful functions
- [ ] Verify on Linux and Windows CI

changed_files.patch:
@@ -60,4 +60,27 @@
 for i in range(len):
     print(result[i])
 
+print('Testing stateful functions...')
+
+N = 4
+state = xtract.xtract_last_n_state_new(N)
+result = xtract.doubleArray(N)
+
+for i in range(N):
+    result[i] = 0.0
+
+data = xtract.doubleArray(1)
+data[0] = 42.0
+# N.B. data is a single sample; N is the window size, not the size of data
+xtract.xtract_last_n(state, data, N, None, result)
+
+print('last_n after one value: %.1f' % result[N - 1])
+
+data[0] = 99.0
+xtract.xtract_last_n(state, data, N, None, result)
+
+print('last_n after two values: %.1f, %.1f' % (result[N - 2], result[N - 1]))
+
+xtract.xtract_last_n_state_delete(state)
+
 print('\nFinished!\n')

commit_summaries.message:
feat: expose stateful functions in SWIG bindings

Remove %ignore directives for xtract_last_n, xtract_last_n_state_new,
and xtract_last_n_state_delete. SWIG handles the opaque
xtract_last_n_state pointer natively — no special typemaps needed.

Add stateful function tests to both Python and Java test scripts
verifying create, push values, read history, and delete.

Closes #59

fix: clarify xtract_last_n usage in test comments

N is the window size (output length), not the size of data.
data is always a single sample. This differs from every other
function in the library where N is the input size.
